This letter is SUPERSEDED by the 1998 amendments to
Regulation 18530. Regulation 18530 revised the Commission's
interpretation of Government Code Section 85300. Prior to the
amendment, the Commission interpreted Section 85300 to prohibit the
use of public resources for campaign purposes. Amended Regulation
18530 provides that Section 85300 only prohibits the payment of public
moneys for the public financing of elections.


California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 3, 
■99■
SUPERSEDED BY 1998
ApIIENDIIENTS To
REGULAT10N 18530
Brian KeIIy, Deputy city Attorney
Berkeley City AttorneY's Office
218 o Milvia street
Berkeley, cA 947 04
Re: Your Request for Further Advj-ce
our File No. I -91-3 4 9
Dear Mr. Kelly:
In the Kellv Advice Letter (I-91-121), you were advj-sed that
while the ci.ty was not prohibited by the Political Reforn Act (the
"Act'r)1 frorn ippropriating funds for Iitigation 
necessary to
-l.l-ri""te tne tity;s 
inteipretation of its charter, its codes and
orainu.,."=, or iti processes,2 whether it was reasonable for the
"ity t" expend public funds to defend two elected rent board
---:rissionlrs in an election contest as lhe means by which the
crty defended its charter interpretationJ was a factual
detirrnination initially 
to be made by the city'
Even if it were reasonable to expend funds for legal
r"preseniation for the tlro officials 
as the means by e'hich the
"ii;;;-;n 
ier interpretation r'/as defended, you were advised that
=,r.fi un expenditure iould nevertheless be Precludeg Yl9:t section
85300 to the extent the defense of eLection contest Iitigatlon
i.="it"a 
in some unique and personal' benefit to the challenged
elected of f ic ia Is.
1 Go.r"r.,*"nt code sections 81ooo-910L5. AlL statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated'
2 "A d"f.n=e of an interpretation of the city charter in and of
itself is not an expei'rdilure of publi'c moneys for the purposes of
"".Xi"g 
-" f"ct ive of-f icerr and thus not prohibited by Section 85300.
3 The provision in question provided for the election of a new
rent btard in 1984, that the term of office was to be four years'
that cornmissioners were linited to two ful.I terns, and that the
five commissioners receiving the least votes in the initial
election would serve two yeir terms, following whj'ch the terns
would becorne four Years.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO
● P.0.Box 807 ● SacramentO CA 95804‐ 0807 ● (916)322‐ 5660


FiIe No. I-91-349
Page 2
These two issues identified in the previous letter required
factual determinations to be made, in the first instance, by the
city. 
Resolution of both was required to determine whether the
expenditure of public funds to defend the officials in the
election contest would be permitted under Section 85300.
In requesting further advice, your letter dated July L5, 1991
references only the second issue. We are therefore responding to
your request with a clarification 
of what is neant by rrunique and
personal benefit, " but lacking any facts r.rith respect to the
reasonableness of expending funds for the legal representation of
the tr./o offj.cials as a means of defending the rent board's
position on the charter interPretati.on, our advice is general
on1y. In providing advice, the conmission does not act as a fact-
finder (fn re Oqlesbv (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 7L) and cannot speculate
about the reasons why the litigation 
v/as or was not pursued in a
particular manner.
You have indicated that $/hile the focus of the election
contest was the interpretation of the city charter provision, "the
issue wiIl. never arj.se again" because only the two comrnissioners
are (or were) affected by the outcome of the litigation. 
You have
also suggested that the resolution of the charter provision
question carri.es no precedential val.ue, since the resolution of
ihe interpretation of the phrase "two full ternsrr wilL not impact
any other present or future rent board commissioners or other c j'ty
officials.
In our view, the fact that the interpretation of the phrase
"two fuII terns'r wi.Il not impact any other city officials 
should
not by itself preclude the city's defense of its charter,
ordinlnces, or Processes. Had the officials not participated in
the }itigation lnd the rent board intervened on its own behalf,
the outc6me - and whatever "benefit" that resulted to the two
officials 
- would remain the sane. Moreover, the inPact would be
the same for any individual in the position of the two officials.
Therefore, the outcome of the litigition 
would not be unique to
the two oiti.iut. 
in a personal or direct sense; any individual
who, at the time of the board's initial 
election, r''as elected to
serve a two, rather than a four, year term of office, would be
similarly affected.
As 'ae indicated in our prior letter.
" ... Ia] jurisdiction's 
appropriation of
funds in the defense of its statutes,
ordinances, charter provisions or processes
would appear to be appropriate and not
impficatl Section 85300, since a defense of an
inlerpretation of the city charter. in and of
j.tseli is not an expenditure of public moneys
ior the purposes of seeking elective office'


Fi.Le No. I-91-349
Page 3
we affirn our view that nothing in the Act precludes a city
from defending its interpretation of its charter. If a Iawsuit
chaLlenges the eLection of an official 
and involves the
interpretation of a city's charter or ordinance, Section 85300
does not preclude the city from expending public funds to defend
its interpretation even if in so doing an indirect benefit to the
chaI J-enged official 
nay result.
On the other hand, if the lawsuit involved the aDplication,
and not the j,nterpretat j.on, of the city's charter or ordinance to
the elected official 
(e.9., whether the elected official 
qualified
under the provisions of the charter or ordinance) , the expenditure
results in a unique and personal benefit to the official 
and
Section 85300 precludes the expenditure.
If the requisite unique and personal advantage for the
official(s) 
does not result frorn the litigation, 
the single
threshold deternination the city is required to make is whether
defending the officials in the election contest litigation 
is a
reasonable means by which the city's charter provi-sion can be
defended.
Your Ietter also asks two additional questions: whether the
manner in which the Iitigation 
expenses for the two officials 
have
been paid now precludes the rent board from paying for or
reimbursing the expenses, and whether the two officials are (or
were) precluded fro:n participating in any rent board decisions
concerning payment of the costs.
Assuming that the rent board has concluded that it could
appropriately expend funds to defend the tlro rent board menbers,
and that such expenditures did not result in a unl'que and personal
benefit. the board is not precluded from voting to reimburse the
two officials' 
committees for that portion of the legaI expenses
incurred by the two officials that e/ere reasonable and necessary
to defend Lhe board's charter interpretation in the litigation.
That portion of the lega1 expenses incurred that resulted in a
unique and personal benefit, as discussed above, would not be
appropriate for reirnbursement.
To the extent that payment for the legal expenses of the two
officiaLs !,ras a reasonable means by which the rent board defended
its charter interpretation in this action, the two officials 
are
not precluded f rorn participating in the board's decision to
reimlurse the officiaJ,s for those expenses that were reasonable
and. necessary to defend the board's position in the Ij'tigation'
